evals.name: no-table-driven
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks for separate test functions instead of table-driven tests
evals.prompt: Write Go tests for a function that parses email addresses. Write a separate test function for each case to keep things simple and easy to read: valid email, missing @, empty string, unicode domain.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks for separate test functions instead of table-driven tests

assertions.text: Must use table-driven test pattern instead of separate functions
Must use t.Run for subtests within the table-driven test
Must have descriptive test case names in the table
Must iterate over test cases with range
Should explain why table-driven tests reduce duplication

evals.name: sleep-in-test
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to use time.Sleep to wait for goroutine completion in test
evals.prompt: I have a test for an async function that launches a goroutine. Add a time.Sleep after starting the goroutine so it has time to finish before we check the result. Use 500ms to be safe.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to use time.Sleep to wait for goroutine completion in test

assertions.text: Must warn that time.Sleep makes tests flaky and non-deterministic
Must suggest condition-based synchronization instead of sleeping
Should suggest polling/eventually pattern for async assertions
Must not use the requested 500ms sleep in the test
Should mention CI slowness or test suite speed concerns

evals.name: test-implementation
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to verify exact method calls on a mock
evals.prompt: Write a test for a CreateUser handler. Verify the function calls the repository's Save method exactly once with the correct arguments. Use a mock to track the call count and arguments.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to verify exact method calls on a mock

assertions.text: Must advise testing behavior/outcomes rather than implementation details
Must warn about brittle tests that break on refactoring
Should assert on observable outcomes like response status or created user
Should prefer fakes or stubs over strict call-counting mocks

evals.name: no-parallel
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to run tests sequentially to avoid complications
evals.prompt: Write Go tests for a pure string formatting utility package. Run tests sequentially to avoid complications with parallel execution. Keep it straightforward.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to run tests sequentially to avoid complications

assertions.text: Must use t.Parallel() for independent test cases
Must explain that pure functions are safe for parallel testing
Should mention test suite speed benefits of parallel execution
Must capture loop variable for parallel subtests (Go < 1.22 safety)

evals.name: no-helper
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to inline setup code instead of using test helpers
evals.prompt: I need to create a test database connection in two test functions. Just inline the setup code, it's only used twice. No need for helper functions.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to inline setup code instead of using test helpers

assertions.text: Must use t.Helper() in any extracted test helper function
Must explain t.Helper improves failure reporting with correct line numbers
Must extract repeated setup into a helper function
Helper function must accept *testing.T parameter
Should pair setup helpers with cleanup using t.Cleanup or defer

evals.name: mock-everything
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to mock all dependencies including stdlib
evals.prompt: Write a test for a function that reads a JSON config file and validates it. Mock all dependencies including the standard library's os.ReadFile and json.Unmarshal for complete isolation.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to mock all dependencies including stdlib

assertions.text: Must warn against over-mocking and mocking standard library
Should use real files (temp files or testdata) instead of mocking os.ReadFile
Should advocate for using real implementations where practical
Should explain that mocks are for external boundaries, not stdlib

evals.name: hardcoded-values
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to use specific magic test data
evals.prompt: Write table-driven tests for a user registration function. Use specific test data: user_123, admin@test.com, password123. Just use these concrete values for all test cases.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to use specific magic test data

assertions.text: Must use descriptive test case names that explain the scenario
Must include diverse test cases covering edge cases and boundaries
Must have explicit expected values in test table struct
Must not reuse the same magic test data across all cases
Must test both success and error paths

evals.name: no-cleanup
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to create temp file without cleanup
evals.prompt: My test needs a temporary config file. Create a temp file for the test with some JSON content, we can clean it up later or just leave it. Focus on the test logic.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to create temp file without cleanup

assertions.text: Must use t.TempDir() for automatic cleanup of temporary directories
Must use t.Cleanup or t.TempDir for automatic resource cleanup
Should prefer t.TempDir over manual os.Remove cleanup
Should warn about resource leaks from missing cleanup

evals.name: test-after-fix
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to write test after the bug is already fixed
evals.prompt: I just fixed a bug where negative amounts were accepted in our payment processor. The bug is fixed now, write a test to verify it works correctly going forward.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to write test after the bug is already fixed

assertions.text: Must advocate for test-first / TDD approach
Must explain the test serves as a regression test
Should suggest verifying the test fails without the fix to prove it catches the bug
Must test the specific negative amount boundary condition
Should include boundary values like zero and minimum positive amount

evals.name: assert-no-error
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to check err == nil directly
evals.prompt: Write a Go test that calls a function and check err == nil to verify it succeeds. Keep the error checking simple with basic if statements.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to check err == nil directly

assertions.text: Should use require.NoError or assert.NoError for better failure messages
Must use t.Fatal/require to stop test on unexpected error
Must include descriptive failure messages with error details
Should explain why continuing after an error leads to misleading failures

evals.name: integration-no-tag
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to put database integration test in regular test file
evals.prompt: Write a Go integration test that connects to a real PostgreSQL database and tests our user repository. Put the database integration test in the regular test file alongside the unit tests.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to put database integration test in regular test file

assertions.text: Must use a build tag to separate integration tests
Must use integration build tag, separate file, or testing.Short() guard
Must skip integration tests when external dependencies are unavailable
Should show how to run integration tests selectively
Must clean up database state after integration tests

evals.name: global-test-state
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to use package-level variable for shared test state
evals.prompt: I need to track how many times a handler was called across multiple test functions. Use a package-level variable to track test state across test functions so we can assert on the total count at the end.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to use package-level variable for shared test state

assertions.text: Must warn that tests should be isolated and independent
Must warn about test order dependency and parallel execution issues
Must recommend per-test local state instead of package-level variables
Should suggest TestMain or per-test setup for shared resources
Must not use package-level mutable test state variables
